course, much depends upon the condition
in which the teams are found, when they

the year, no county is in anything like first-
class form—particularly where football is
concerned.  We have recently heard com-
phaints of poor form from such big guns as
Kerry and Wexford, so that lesser lights
can scarcely be much better of. But this
Cork v Louth tic appears to have called for
an unusually strong cffort on the part of
the teams, in view of the rivalry engen-
dered when Louth defeated Cork in the
same event not many months ago. The
Corkmen have taken the matter to heart,
apparently, and are determined to retrieve
their position. Their capacity as foot-
ballers is undoubtedly very great, but, bar-
ring a tremendous improvement upon their
past form, they will scarcely outpoint the
Louthmen.  However, fate may decide
otherwise, and in_cither eveat, there will
be very little margin between the counties.
There should be a first-class football display
when the teams meet, and to the Gaclic
enthusiast nothing better will be available
for a_ considerable time than the battle
listed for Croke Park on Sunday next.
